title: Scheduled-Task Persistence + Defender Kill
briefing: $TARGET logged Event ID 4698 — $USER1 created a scheduled task under a legit-looking path (\Microsoft\Windows\UpdateOrchestrator\...) whose action is an encoded PowerShell command. Moments later Windows Defender raised EID 5001: real-time protection was turned off on the same host. Together these are an attacker nailing down persistence and then blinding the endpoint's AV so the payload runs unimpeded. Confirm both, tie them to one actor, and decide what to trust on this host.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> agent.name: $TARGET. rule.id: 100139 is the 4698 task-create (read win.eventdata.taskName and taskContent — the encoded -enc powershell is the tell). rule.id: 100170 is the 5001 Defender-disabled event. Same host, back-to-back = persistence + defense evasion by one actor. A masquerading task path (UpdateOrchestrator) hiding an encoded command is not a real Windows update.

options: Persistence via scheduled task plus Defender being disabled to evade AV
questions.answer: Persistence via scheduled task plus Defender being disabled to evade AV
questions.explain: A scheduled task running encoded PowerShell (persistence) immediately followed by real-time protection being switched off (defense evasion) is an attacker entrenching and blinding the host.
Both the 4698 and the Defender 5001 events land on $TARGET; other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: What makes the scheduled task suspicious rather than a real update?
options: It masquerades under an update path but runs an encoded PowerShell command
questions.answer: It masquerades under an update path but runs an encoded PowerShell command
questions.explain: Legit Windows update tasks don't launch base64 -enc PowerShell. The UpdateOrchestrator path is masquerading (T1036) to blend in.

questions.prompt: Why disable Defender right after creating the task?
options: So the payload the task runs isn't detected or blocked by AV
questions.answer: So the payload the task runs isn't detected or blocked by AV
questions.explain: Killing real-time protection (T1562.001) lets the persistence payload execute without AV interference — the two actions are one operation.

questions.prompt: Which account created the scheduled task?

options: T1562.001 Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
answer: T1562.001 Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
questions.explain: Scheduled-task persistence (T1053.005), disabling Defender (T1562.001), and the fake update path (T1036). No brute force.

options: Isolate $TARGET and delete the rogue scheduled task
Re-enable and force a full Defender scan; check tamper-protection is on
Reset $USER1 and review what it touched
Hunt other hosts for the same task path / Defender-off events
Close it — Defender toggling off is normal
answer: Isolate $TARGET and delete the rogue scheduled task
Re-enable and force a full Defender scan; check tamper-protection is on
Reset $USER1 and review what it touched
Hunt other hosts for the same task path / Defender-off events
questions.explain: Remove the persistence, restore and rescan the AV (and enable tamper protection so it can't be silenced again), rotate the abusing account, and sweep the fleet for the same TTP.
